verificationNote: Sector-specific requirements are constructed from IEC 62443, NIST SP 800-82 Rev 3, and NACSA Act 854 obligations for the Malaysian water sector. They have not been endorsed by SPAN (National Water Services Commission) or NACSA. The Oldsmar, Florida water plant attack (2021) and similar incidents inform the threat model for this sector.
nacsaSectorLead: SPAN — Suruhanjaya Perkhidmatan Air Negara (National Water Services Commission)
sectorOtCharacteristics: Direct public health impact — water quality tampering could affect large populations
Chemical dosing systems (chlorine, fluoride) are highest-consequence OT assets
Many remote unmanned pump stations with cellular connectivity
High ratio of legacy PLCs and RTUs with limited authentication capability
24/7 operational requirement with real-time process monitoring
Historically low OT cybersecurity investment compared to energy sector

requirements.name: Chemical dosing system isolation and integrity
requirements.description: Chemical dosing control systems (chlorine, coagulant, fluoride) are the highest-consequence OT assets in water treatment. Unauthorised manipulation of chemical dosing setpoints or control logic could cause public health harm. The Oldsmar attack (February 2021) involved remote manipulation of sodium hydroxide dosing to a dangerous level via TeamViewer left open on an HMI.

legal.summary: Chemical dosing systems represent the highest-consequence OT assets in the water sector. NACSA s18 requires measures appropriate to the nature and sensitivity of NCII. SPAN may issue specific requirements for chemical dosing security as sector codes are developed.

technical.summary: Chemical dosing controllers on dedicated isolated segment. No remote access to chemical dosing PLCs except via approved PAM-controlled session with dual-person awareness. Hardware interlocks independent of software. Chemical dosing setpoint changes logged and alerted.
actions: Chemical dosing PLCs on dedicated network segment, further isolated from main SCADA LAN — no remote access path from internet
Eliminate any general remote desktop tools (TeamViewer, AnyDesk, VNC) from chemical dosing HMI — replace with organisation-controlled jump server with MFA if remote visibility is genuinely required
Implement hardware-based setpoint limits independent of PLC software: high-limit trip switches at chemical injection pumps that cannot be overridden from software alone
Log all changes to chemical dosing setpoints: who, when, from where, old value, new value — alert OT security team immediately on setpoint change outside approved maintenance window
Physical access: chemical dosing control cabinets locked; key access tightly controlled; operations manager holds keys
OT network monitoring covering chemical dosing segment — alert on any new connection to chemical dosing PLCs

governance.summary: Chemical dosing control system security reviewed annually and after any security incident. Hardware interlock testing documented.
actions: Annual chemical dosing security review
Hardware interlock test documented at each annual maintenance shutdown
Chemical dosing setpoint change procedure defines approval process and logging requirement

evidenceItems: Absence of generic remote desktop tools on dosing HMI (configuration evidence)
Physical access control for dosing cabinets

slMapping.sl2: Chemical dosing PLCs on isolated segment; no TeamViewer/VNC; setpoint change logging
slMapping.sl3: Hardware interlocks independent of software; jump server with MFA for any remote access; OT monitoring covering dosing segment; immediate alert on setpoint change
slMapping.sl4: Dual-person approval for chemical dosing setpoint changes; cryptographic integrity verification of dosing PLC programs; real-time alerting to operations and OT security team

requirements.name: Remote pump station and RTU security
requirements.description: Water distribution networks include many remote, unmanned pump stations connected via cellular (4G/5G) or MPLS WAN. These remote sites have limited physical security, often use legacy PLCs/RTUs with weak authentication, and represent a large attack surface. Compromise of remote pump stations could disrupt water distribution pressure.

legal.summary: Remote pump stations are NCII OT assets. NACSA s18 requires measures appropriate to their criticality. NACSA s21 risk assessment must include remote sites.

technical.summary: Remote pump stations: encrypted WAN connection (VPN or MPLS), no direct internet exposure of RTU/PLC IP addresses, physical tamper detection, cellular modem in separate locked enclosure from PLC cabinet.
actions: All remote pump station communications over encrypted VPN or dedicated MPLS — no direct internet access to RTU/PLC IP addresses
Cellular modems for WAN connectivity configured with firewall rules: only permit inbound from SCADA IP, no inbound management access from internet
Remote stations: cellular modem in separate locked enclosure from PLC — if cellular modem is compromised, physical access still needed to reach PLC
Physical tamper detection on RTU/PLC cabinets at remote sites — alert on cabinet open
Legacy RTUs with no authentication capability: compensating controls are VPN encryption at WAN layer and physical access control; document as exception
Periodic physical inspection of remote sites on schedule based on criticality (monthly for high-criticality pumping stations; quarterly for distribution RTU sites)
SCADA operator alert if remote site stops communicating — could indicate intentional disruption or network attack

governance.summary: Remote site security assessment included in annual risk review.
actions: Annual audit of remote site WAN configuration — verify VPN/MPLS in use; no direct internet exposure
Remote site inspection schedule maintained; missed inspections escalated
Exception register for legacy RTUs without authentication

slMapping.sl2: VPN/MPLS WAN; cellular modem firewall; physical cabinet locks; inspection schedule
slMapping.sl3: Tamper detection on RTU cabinets with SCADA alert; modem separated from PLC; legacy RTU exceptions documented
slMapping.sl4: Continuous remote monitoring of RTU communication integrity; automated alert on anomalous command patterns from remote sites

requirements.name: Water quality monitoring data integrity
requirements.description: Online water quality sensors (turbidity, pH, residual chlorine, pressure) feed directly into SCADA for automated process adjustments. If sensor data is falsified or sensor communications are manipulated, automated controls could apply incorrect chemical doses or fail to detect contamination events.

legal.summary: Water quality data integrity is a public health obligation. NACSA s18 applies. Contamination events with OT cyber causation would also trigger NACSA s26 notification obligations.
legal.owner: OT Security / Water Quality Manager / Operations

technical.summary: Online water quality sensor data validated against expected ranges in SCADA. Anomalous sensor readings that would trigger significant process adjustment require operator confirmation. Manual water quality sampling runs in parallel as independent verification.
actions: Implement SCADA validation logic: sensor readings outside defined operating ranges trigger operator confirmation before automated action
Cross-validate water quality sensor readings against manual laboratory results — significant divergence triggers investigation
OT network monitoring on sensor communication segments — alert on new device appearing or unusual communication patterns from sensor gateways
Sensor communication security: IEC 62443 SR-5.1 zoning applied to sensor network — sensors on dedicated segment, not directly on SCADA LAN
Historian records all sensor readings with timestamp — anomaly in sensor data recordable and investigable post-incident

governance.summary: Water quality data integrity procedure integrates OT security and water quality management.
actions: Procedure defines response to sensor anomaly: manual sampling initiated, SCADA operator informed, OT security alerted if cyber cause suspected
Annual review of SCADA validation thresholds against operational experience

evidenceItems: Cross-validation procedure between online sensors and manual sampling
OT monitoring coverage of sensor network
Anomaly response procedure for sensor data integrity events

slMapping.sl2: Sensor validation in SCADA; manual sampling cross-check; sensor network segmented from SCADA LAN
slMapping.sl3: Automated alert on sensor anomaly; OT monitoring on sensor network; divergence triggers investigation procedure
slMapping.sl4: Cryptographic integrity of sensor communications; real-time divergence analysis across multiple sensors; automated contamination alert

sectorSpecificThreats.threat: Remote pump station compromise via WAN
